title: Detecting Connected Components in a Graph: Incorrect DFS

statement.markdown: Given an adjacency list representation of a graph, implement a function to detect and return the connected components of the graph. You should not consider the ordering in which the components are returned.
statement.inputFormat: The graph is represented as an adjacency list where each key is a vertex and its corresponding value is a list of adjacent vertices.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return a list of lists where each sublist contains a connected component of the graph.
statement.notes: Note that the graph is undirected and unweighted.

examples.input:
Example input (matching the stdin format that main wrapper expects): 
3
[0,1], [0,2], [1,2]
examples.explanation: The graph is fully connected, so there is only one connected component.

hints.text: Start with a given node and perform DFS on the graph.

editorial.title: Correct DFS Traversal Order in a Graph
editorial.intuition: The correct approach is to start a DFS traversal from each unvisited node and include all nodes in the same DFS traversal as part of the same connected component.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute force solution is to perform DFS traversal from each node and keep track of visited nodes using recursion.
editorial.betterSolution: Use DFS and mark visited nodes to ensure we do not visit the same node in different DFS traversals.
editorial.optimalSolution: Perform DFS from each unvisited node and use a set to keep track of visited nodes.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the algorithm can be proved using the fact that DFS traverses the graph level by level, and if two nodes are in the same level, they are connected.
editorial.dryRun: Start a DFS traversal from node 0, visit node 1, and then visit node 2.
commonMistakes: Mistaking a disconnected graph for a connected one
Mistaking a fully connected graph for an incomplete graph

functionSignature.python: def solve(self, edges: list, n: int) -> list:
